---
title: "Native performance and mmap readers"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Native performance and mmap readers}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
library(dafr)
```

# Why native?

`dafr` is a pure R + C++ port of the Julia `DataAxesFormats.jl` package.
Compared to a Julia-facade wrapper, native has:

- No `JuliaCall` copy tax on cross-language boundaries.
- mmap-backed reads for vectors and sparse matrices (no double-buffer).
- OpenMP-parallel query kernels (Sum, Mean, Var, Mode, Quantile, â€¦).
- User-extensible op registry (`register_eltwise`, `register_reduction`).

# Mmap readers

When a FilesDaf is opened read-only, dense vectors and the three payload
arrays of a sparse matrix (`colptr`, `rowval`, `nzval`) are `mmap`'d â€” the
OS maps the on-disk files directly into process memory with no copy.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
fd <- files_daf("/path/to/daf", mode = "r")
x  <- get_vector(fd, "cell", "donor")     # mmap'd â€” no allocation
m  <- get_matrix(fd, "cell", "gene", "UMIs")  # mmap'd dgCMatrix
```

Low-level mmap constructors are also exported for advanced uses:
`mmap_dgCMatrix`, `mmap_int`, `mmap_lgl`, `mmap_real`.

# Parallel kernels

Reductions above a size threshold dispatch to OpenMP. The threshold is
controlled via `options(dafr.kernel_threshold = N)` where `N` is the
minimum element count for parallel execution.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
options(dafr.kernel_threshold = 1e5)   # default 1e6
```

Thread count honours `set_num_threads()` (or the `dafr.num_threads`
option). CRAN-like environments are auto-capped to 2 cores on package
load.

# Performance vs DataAxesFormats.jl

The package is benchmarked against the reference Julia implementation
(`DataAxesFormats.jl`) â€” native Julia in-process, with no R or wrapper
layer â€” using a shared fixture set. Fixtures hash-match across runners,
queries are driven from the same catalog, and each iteration invalidates
the per-query cache so timings reflect real compute rather than a
cache-lookup ceiling.

**Setup** (2026-04-22): `dafr` commit `d6d9a14`, R 4.4.1 with MKL 2024.1
(ILP64), `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`; Julia 1.12.5, OpenBLAS-64, threads =
default. Linux x86_64. 79 queries across four fixtures: `big_sparse`
(dense kernel sweeps on a large CSC matrix), `cells_daf` (51 queries
covering every phrase in the string DSL), `chain_triple` (read paths
through a three-layer chain), `mmap_reopen` (filesystem open + single
read).

**Reading the tables.** In every table `Ratio = dafr / julia` (lower is
better for dafr) and `Speedup = julia / dafr` (higher is better for
dafr). A speedup of `10Ă—` means dafr is ten times faster than Julia.

**Headline.** Of the 79 benchmarked queries, dafr is faster than Julia
on 56, at parity on 12, and slower on 11 (of which 4 exceed a 1.5Ă—
threshold â€” all on the filesystem-open-then-immediately-read micro-test).
On the heavy workloads that dominate real pipelines (sparse-matrix
reductions, grouped reductions), dafr wins by 2Ă—â€“37Ă—.

## Heavy kernels on a large sparse matrix

Dense reductions over a CSC matrix (`big_sparse`, ~100k Ă— 5k, 2 %
density). `dafr`'s OpenMP kernels win decisively even at
`OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`:

| Query                 | dafr median | Julia median | Ratio (R/J) | Speedup (J/R) |
|-----------------------|------------:|-------------:|------------:|--------------:|
| `kernel_var_row`      |     158 ms  |    5.86 s    |       0.03Ă— |         **37Ă—** |
| `kernel_std_row`      |     159 ms  |    5.85 s    |       0.03Ă— |         **37Ă—** |
| `kernel_mean_row`     |     153 ms  |    2.83 s    |       0.05Ă— |         **18Ă—** |
| `kernel_max_row`      |     158 ms  |    2.90 s    |       0.05Ă— |         **18Ă—** |
| `kernel_median_row`   |     190 ms  |    3.50 s    |       0.05Ă— |         **18Ă—** |
| `kernel_quantile_row` |     194 ms  |    3.16 s    |       0.06Ă— |         **16Ă—** |
| `kernel_geomean_row`  |     226 ms  |    3.45 s    |       0.07Ă— |         **15Ă—** |
| `kernel_sum_row`      |     184 ms  |    2.73 s    |       0.07Ă— |         **15Ă—** |
| `kernel_mode_row`     |     872 ms  |    6.31 s    |       0.14Ă— |          **7Ă—** |
| `kernel_max_col`      |     154 ms  |     683 ms   |       0.23Ă— |          **4Ă—** |
| `kernel_sum_col`      |     162 ms  |     713 ms   |       0.23Ă— |          **4Ă—** |
| `kernel_mean_col`     |     161 ms  |     643 ms   |       0.25Ă— |          **4Ă—** |

## Grouped reductions

Same fixture, grouping a vector into bins before reducing:

| Query                  | dafr median | Julia median | Ratio (R/J) | Speedup (J/R) |
|------------------------|------------:|-------------:|------------:|--------------:|
| `grouped_g2_max_100`   |     113 ms  |    2.94 s    |       0.04Ă— |         **26Ă—** |
| `grouped_g2_mean_100`  |     215 ms  |    3.16 s    |       0.07Ă— |         **15Ă—** |
| `grouped_g2_mean_1000` |     287 ms  |    3.87 s    |       0.07Ă— |         **13Ă—** |
| `grouped_g2_sum_100`   |     215 ms  |    2.88 s    |       0.07Ă— |         **13Ă—** |
| `grouped_g3_mean_100`  |     169 ms  |    2.11 s    |       0.08Ă— |         **12Ă—** |
| `grouped_g3_sum_100`   |     172 ms  |    1.99 s    |       0.09Ă— |         **12Ă—** |
| `grouped_g3_max_100`   |     192 ms  |    2.07 s    |       0.09Ă— |         **11Ă—** |
| `grouped_g3_mean_1000` |    1.07 s   |    2.04 s    |       0.52Ă— |          **2Ă—** |

## String-DSL queries on a metacell-scale store

51 queries covering every phrase in the query language (`cells_daf`, a
subset of `example_cells_daf()`). Times are sub-millisecond to
low-millisecond for both runners; dafr is at parity or faster on most,
modestly slower on a handful of axis-mask / lookup-composition phrases:

| Summary                    | Count |
|----------------------------|------:|
| dafr faster (ratio < 0.9Ă—) |    23 |
| parity (0.9Ă— â€“ 1.1Ă—)       |    10 |
| dafr slower (ratio > 1.1Ă—) |    18 |
| worst ratio                | 1.73Ă— |

No `cells_daf` query exceeded its 2Ă— threshold.

## Chain queries

Reading through a three-daf chain (`chain_triple`):

| Query               | dafr median | Julia median | Ratio (R/J) | Speedup (J/R) |
|---------------------|------------:|-------------:|------------:|--------------:|
| `chain_read_vector` |     694 Âµs  |    1.15 ms   |       0.60Ă— |        **1.7Ă—** |
| `chain_read_matrix` |     931 Âµs  |    1.09 ms   |       0.85Ă— |        **1.2Ă—** |
| `chain_read_scalar` |     656 Âµs  |     398 Âµs   |       1.65Ă— |         0.6Ă—  |
| `chain_reduce`      |    2.80 ms  |    1.67 ms   |       1.68Ă— |         0.6Ă—  |

## Open + first-read latency (only place dafr loses)

On the `mmap_reopen` fixture â€” open a FilesDaf and immediately read one
property â€” dafr is consistently slower than Julia. All four are within a
few milliseconds absolute, but the per-call overhead is higher:

| Query                    | dafr median | Julia median | Ratio (R/J) | Speedup (J/R) |
|--------------------------|------------:|-------------:|------------:|--------------:|
| `mmap_open_read_axis`    |    2.11 ms  |     794 Âµs   |       2.66Ă— |         0.38Ă— |
| `mmap_open_read_matrix`  |    4.09 ms  |    1.66 ms   |       2.46Ă— |         0.41Ă— |
| `mmap_open_read_vector`  |    2.96 ms  |    1.54 ms   |       1.93Ă— |         0.52Ă— |
| `mmap_open_read_scalar`  |    1.00 ms  |     642 Âµs   |       1.56Ă— |         0.64Ă— |

The gap is per-call: JSON descriptor parsing, `stat`s, and the one-shot
mmap setup cost more per file in R than in Julia. For workloads that
open a store once and then stream many reads (the common case), this
cost amortises to zero â€” see the kernel tables above. For
open-heavy workflows, keep the `FilesDaf` handle alive across queries.

## Reproducing the numbers

The bake-off harness lives under `benchmarks/` (R runner) and
`benchmarks/julia/` (Julia runner). Both runners consume the same query
catalog and fixture set and emit CSVs with identical schemas so the
comparison step is a simple join. See `benchmarks/README.md` for the
full workflow.
